skill.name: solana-dev
skill.description: Use when user asks to "build a Solana dapp", "write an Anchor program", "create a token", "debug Solana errors", "set up wallet connection", "test my Solana program", "deploy to devnet", or "explain Solana concepts" (rent, accounts, PDAs, CPIs, etc.). End-to-end Solana development playbook covering wallet connection, Anchor/Pinocchio programs, Codama client generation, LiteSVM/Mollusk/Surfpool testing, and security checklists. Integrates with the Solana MCP server for live documentation search. Prefers framework-kit (@solana/client + @solana/react-hooks) for UI, wallet-standard-first connection (incl. ConnectorKit), @solana/kit for client/RPC code, and @solana/web3-compat for legacy boundaries.
skill.summary: Build and debug Solana dapps with Anchor programs, wallet connections, and comprehensive testing frameworks.
skill.author: solana-foundation

security_audit.summary: This skill is a legitimate Solana developer documentation reference from Solana Foundation. All static findings are false positives - the detected patterns are markdown documentation showing CLI commands, error messages, and code examples, not executable malicious code. The skill provides development tooling references and integrates with official Solana MCP server for documentation search.

medium_findings.title: Documentation Patterns Misidentified as Executable Code
medium_findings.description: Static analyzer detected 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' patterns in markdown documentation files. These are documentation showing CLI commands (anchor build, solana-test-validator) and shell examples, not actual code execution. The backtick syntax is used in markdown code blocks to show command examples for developers.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Markdown files contain only documentation text, not executable code. The 'backtick execution' pattern matches markdown code block syntax for displaying CLI commands.

medium_findings.title: Cryptographic Algorithm References in Documentation
medium_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 'weak cryptographic algorithm' patterns in documentation files. These are references to encryption methods (AES, SHA256) and their usage patterns documented for developers, not actual vulnerable cryptographic implementations.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Skill is documentation for using Solana's confidential transfers feature, which uses proper encryption. References are educational content about cryptographic implementation.

low_findings.title: Network URL References in Documentation
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs detected in documentation are references to official Solana documentation, RPC endpoints, and developer resources.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: These are legitimate references to Solana documentation and official developer resources, not suspicious external connections.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Sequences in Migration Documentation
low_findings.description: Path traversal patterns like '../' appear in migration documentation showing file path examples during version upgrades.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Migration guides legitimately show file path changes between versions. No path traversal vulnerability exists in the documentation content.

content.user_title: Build and Debug Solana Dapps with Anchor
content.value_statement: Developers building Solana applications struggle to find comprehensive documentation and proper implementation patterns. This skill provides end-to-end guidance for wallet integration, smart contract development, testing frameworks, and deployment workflows.
actual_capabilities: Generate Anchor program code with proper account validation and error handling
Set up wallet connections using wallet-standard and ConnectorKit
Debug Solana transaction errors and resolve common anchor issues
Create and configure tokens using Token-2022 extensions
Write tests using LiteSVM, Mollusk, or Surfpool frameworks
Deploy programs to devnet, testnet, and mainnet
limitations: Cannot sign transactions or access private keys directly
Requires internet access for Solana RPC endpoints
Testing frameworks require local Solana installation for some features
May need manual verification of complex PDA derivation logic

use_cases.title: Building Token Programs
use_cases.description: Create fungible or non-fungible tokens on Solana with proper metadata, mint authorities, and freeze clauses.
use_cases.target_user: Token developers and NFT project creators

use_cases.title: Debugging Transaction Failures
use_cases.description: Diagnose and resolve common Anchor errors including account conflicts, rent issues, and signature mismatches.

use_cases.title: Setting Up Development Environments
use_cases.description: Configure local validators, install Anchor, and integrate with wallet providers for rapid development iteration.
use_cases.target_user: New Solana developers starting their first projects

prompt_templates.title: Create Basic Token
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a fungible token on Solana with {TOKEN_NAME} symbol and {DECIMALS} decimals. Include a mint authority and freeze authority.

prompt_templates.title: Debug Transaction Error
prompt_templates.prompt: Debug this Solana transaction error: {ERROR_MESSAGE}. The transaction was trying to {ACTION} on account {ACCOUNT_ADDRESS}.

prompt_templates.title: Implement CPI Call
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a cross-program invocation from {SOURCE_PROGRAM} to call {TARGET_PROGRAM} with {ACCOUNTS} accounts and {DATA} instruction data.

prompt_templates.title: Write Program Tests
prompt_templates.prompt: Write comprehensive tests for {PROGRAM_NAME} using {TESTING_FRAMEWORK}. Include happy path, edge cases, and failure scenarios.

output_examples.input: Create a staking program that rewards users with tokens for locking their NFTs
output_examples.output: Implementation plan for a staking smart contract with account validation, reward calculation logic, and withdrawal handlers.

output_examples.input: Debug: Transaction simulation failed with error 'instruction consumed too many compute units'
output_examples.output: Analysis of compute budget usage and optimization suggestions including adding compute units, simplifying validation, and reducing account reads.

best_practices: Always validate PDAs by deriving them with findProgramAddress and checking the resulting address matches expected values
Use explicit account ordering in Anchor instructions and match the IDL definition order
Include proper error types with #[error_code] attributes for better debugging and user feedback
anti_patterns: Do not skip rent-exempt checks when creating accounts - this causes transactions to fail on mainnet
Avoid hardcoding program IDs - always derive them from the Anchor workspace or configuration
Do not use unchecked arithmetic for token amounts - always use safe math operations to prevent overflow exploits

faq.question: What is the difference between Anchor and Pinocchio?
faq.answer: Anchor provides a higher-level framework with derive macros and account validation. Pinocchio is a lower-level SDK closer to the raw BPF bytecode. Use Anchor for most programs, Pinocchio for ultra-optimized cases.

faq.question: How do I calculate program-derived addresses correctly?
faq.answer: Use the bump seed from findProgramAddress and store it in your account. Always validate the PDA by re-deriving it with the stored bump to prevent spoofing attacks.

faq.question: Why is my transaction failing with insufficient funds for rent?
faq.answer: Accounts need to be rent-exempt by maintaining a minimum balance. Use SystemAccount for writable accounts and ensure the payer has enough SOL for the account size.

faq.question: What testing framework should I use?
faq.answer: LiteSVM is fastest for simple programs. Surfpool provides accurate local validator simulation. Mollusk bridges both approaches. Choose based on your testing complexity needs.

faq.question: How do I handle cross-program invocations safely?
faq.answer: Always verify the calling program ID, validate all accounts passed through CPI, and use privilege de-escalation by closing signer accounts after the CPI call completes.

faq.question: What are the mainnet safety considerations?
faq.answer: Use upgradeable programs with a multisig authority, implement pause functionality, add timestamp-based invariants, and always test on devnet and testnet first with real assets.
